metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the blocking `review_code()` call is offloaded off the asyncio event loop in the background worker (e.g., `asyncio.to_thread(review_code, ...)` or `loop.run_in_executor(None, ...)`) so the event loop remains responsive to new webhook deliveries while a review is in progress.
findings.finding: review_code() is fully synchronous (blocking subprocess and LLM calls), but the plan specifies an asyncio background worker with an asyncio.Lock-based single-flight. The plan never specifies how the blocking review_code() call is bridged into the asyncio event loop (e.g., asyncio.to_thread / loop.run_in_executor). Calling it directly in an async task will block the entire event loop, preventing the receiver from processing any other requests during the (potentially minutes-long) review.
findings.location: ## What — 'ACK fast (HTTP 202) and enqueue, then process in a background worker' + Round-12 ACs: 'in-process per-(change_id,revision) single-flight lock'
findings.suggested_fix: Use `await asyncio.to_thread(review_code, diff_text=..., repo_root=..., ref=..., source='local')` in the background worker coroutine, and document this in the relevant AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the blocking `review_code()` call is offloaded from the asyncio event loop in the background worker (e.g. `await asyncio.to_thread(review_code, ...)` or `loop.run_in_executor(None, review_code, ...)`), and confirm the asyncio.Lock single-flight pattern works correctly across threads (asyncio locks are not thread-safe — a threading.Lock or a thread-pool + asyncio.Queue pattern may be needed).
findings.finding: review_code() is fully synchronous (blocking subprocess.run + blocking LLM runner calls per code_review.py lines 54 and 192-228), but the plan specifies an asyncio background worker with an asyncio.Lock single-flight — and never specifies how the blocking call is bridged off the asyncio event loop (e.g. asyncio.to_thread or loop.run_in_executor). Calling review_code() directly inside an async task will block the entire event loop for the duration of the review (30s–minutes), preventing the receiver from ACKing any subsequent webhook deliveries and defeating the stated async-ACK-then-process design.
findings.location: What section — 'ACK fast (HTTP 202) and enqueue, then process in a background worker'; Round-12 ACs — 'An in-process per-(change_id,revision) single-flight lock … asyncio.Lock'
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, add: 'The background worker runs review_code() via `await asyncio.to_thread(adapter.code_review_decision, ...)` to avoid blocking the event loop. The single-flight per-(change_id, revision) uses an asyncio.Lock guarding the async wrapper, with the blocking call isolated inside the thread.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve whether the SQLite dedup store will use `aiosqlite` (non-blocking async) or synchronous `sqlite3` wrapped in `asyncio.to_thread`/`run_in_executor`; document this choice (prior art: `aiosqlite`) so the implementation is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The plan specifies SQLite (sqlite3) for the dedup store with WAL mode, shared between the asyncio webhook worker and the asyncio backfill reconciler. Python's stdlib sqlite3 module is synchronous/blocking. Calling it directly from an asyncio context without a thread executor will block the event loop on every dedup read/write. The established prior art for async SQLite in Python is aiosqlite (or explicit run_in_executor wrapping), which the plan neither mentions nor justifies omitting.
findings.location: ## What — 'a small local SQLite file'; Round-12 ACs: 'SQLite schema is (change_id,revision) PK in WAL mode'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the ADR AC to explicitly require the ADR to record the `code_review_decision(diff_text, repo_root, ref) -> {decision, message, findings}` signature and the error→BLOCK fail-closed rule as normative constraints b744-WS6 must conform to.
findings.finding: The ADR acceptance criterion ('records the bot/label/event/auth contract and the seam b744-WS6 must conform to') does not explicitly require the ADR to capture the adapter's formal function signature `code_review_decision(diff_text, repo_root, ref) -> {decision: PASS|BLOCK, message, findings}` and its fail-closed error rule — the most operationally critical part of the seam contract that b744-WS6 must conform to.
findings.location: AC: 'Seam documented. docs/adr/0009-review-bot-pipe.md records the bot/label/event/auth contract and the seam b744-WS6 must conform to.'
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC: 'docs/adr/0009-review-bot-pipe.md records the bot/label/event/auth contract, the adapter signature (code_review_decision(diff_text, repo_root, ref) -> {decision: PASS|BLOCK, message, findings}), the error→BLOCK fail-closed rule, and the verdict→label mapping — all as normative constraints b744-WS6 must conform to.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the cursor's storage mechanism, schema (e.g., a `cursor` table in voted.db with `plugin TEXT, last_event_id TEXT`), and behavior on absence/corruption (fall back to 'now minus poll window', log+alarm, never full-history scan).
findings.finding: The events-log reconciler cursor's persistence mechanism is unspecified: the plan says 'persisted cursor (last events-log event id)' but does not define where it is stored (same voted.db? a separate file? in-memory reset on restart?), its schema, or its behavior on corruption or process restart.
findings.location: Round-11 AC — 'Reconciler is resumable+idempotent via a persisted cursor (last events-log event id)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `cursor(plugin TEXT PRIMARY KEY, last_event_id TEXT, updated_at INTEGER)` table to the voted.db schema. On absence, default to `now - 10min` (safe bounded backfill). On corruption, log+alarm and use the same default — never scan unbounded history.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and name the exact events-log plugin query parameters used by reconcile.py (time-range parameter name and format, max-results/pagination parameter, and how the persisted cursor event-id maps to a query parameter) so the gap-window enumeration is unambiguously implementable without out-of-band research.
findings.finding: The reconciler's events-log REST integration is specified only at the endpoint-path level (GET /a/plugins/events-log/events/ with trailing slash). The events-log plugin's query interface — specifically the time-range parameter name (t=, since=, or a timestamp form), the max-results / pagination parameter (n=), and whether cursor-based pagination is supported — is never named in the plan. Without these, the gap-window enumeration is unimplementable without out-of-band research, and a wrong parameter name causes every poll to silently return empty (dropped webhooks are never recovered).
findings.location: Round-9 remediation — reconciler query mechanism; Round-12 — 'events-log query uses the TRAILING SLASH'; Round-11 AC — 'Reconciler is resumable+idempotent via a persisted cursor (last events-log event id)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the blocking-severity threshold config key (e.g. `REVIEW_BOT_BLOCKING_SEVERITY`) in the Round-7 AC and in the ADR seam section, so `adapter.py` and `infra/` SSM provisioning use the same parameter name.
findings.finding: The blocking-severity threshold config key/env-var name is never specified. The plan says 'the threshold is config-driven (env/SSM/config under infra/)' but no key name (e.g. `REVIEW_BOT_BLOCKING_SEVERITY_THRESHOLD`) is given. An implementer writing the adapter and an operator provisioning SSM cannot coordinate on the parameter name.
findings.location: Round-7 remediation section; AC 'The receiver maps review_code()\'s review_result.findings to a vote via the documented severity-threshold rule'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Round-7 AC: 'The threshold is read from env var `REVIEW_BOT_BLOCKING_SEVERITY` (comma-separated list, default `critical,high`); record the key name in `docs/adr/0009-review-bot-pipe.md`.' Mirror this in the `infra/` section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate the severity-threshold decision rule with the Round-11 adapter AC: explicitly state that `code_review_decision()` applies the blocking-threshold rule from Round-7 (BLOCK if any finding.severity ∈ threshold set, else PASS), and add a test case verifying a `medium`-severity-only finding yields PASS with default threshold.
findings.finding: The severity-threshold decision rule (stated in Round-7) is not co-located with the `code_review_decision()` adapter schema defined in Round-11. Round-11 specifies the adapter signature and return type `{decision, message, findings}` but does not reference or embed the threshold rule — the two halves of the same processing protocol live in separate remediation rounds with no cross-reference.
findings.location: Round-7 remediation section (decision rule) vs. Round-11 remediation section (adapter schema and AC)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Round-11 adapter AC, add: 'The adapter applies the severity-threshold rule: BLOCK iff any `finding.severity` is in the configured blocking set (default `{critical, high}`); a finding at `medium` or below yields PASS. Add this scenario to the offline test suite.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the `+MAX` label value config key (e.g. `REVIEW_BOT_LLM_REVIEW_MAX_VOTE`) in the label-mapping section and in ADR-0009, and specify that it must match the `LLM-Review` label's `max` value in `project.config`.
findings.finding: The `+MAX` label value config key is referenced as 'config-driven' but never named. The plan says 'the mapping (and the MAX value / label range, owned by d251 project.config) is config-driven (env/SSM/config under infra/), not hardcoded' — but no env-var or SSM path name is given for where the MAX integer is read from.
findings.location: What section, 'Code-review verdict -> label mapping' bullet; AC 'Vote -> submittability'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the label mapping section: 'MAX value is read from env `REVIEW_BOT_LLM_REVIEW_MAX_VOTE` (integer, default `1`); must match the `max` value configured for the `LLM-Review` label in `project.config`. Record the key in ADR-0009.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider splitting S4b into two child slices: (1) the synchronous pipe (webhook→adapter→vote + dedup + single-flight + ADR) and (2) the async recovery layer (reconciler+cursor + CloudWatch alarm) — each independently verifiable with a live e2e test before the other is started.
findings.finding: As a leaf ticket S4b accumulates ≥18 distinct ACs spanning 6-7 independent implementation tasks (webhook handler+auth, background worker+adapter, SQLite dedup+WAL, in-process single-flight lock, backfill reconciler+cursor, CloudWatch metric+alarm, ADR), making coherent single-session execution unlikely and increasing the risk of partial-done states that leave the pipe in an inconsistent fail-open posture.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria (all rounds combined) / ## Files
findings.suggested_fix: Slice 1 (core pipe): webhook handler, adapter.py, SQLite dedup (write-on-success), single-flight lock, outbound vote, base64-decode, live e2e happy-path AC. Slice 2 (resilience): reconcile.py + cursor, CloudWatch metric/alarm, live e2e 'dropped webhook recovered' AC. Slice 1 already proves the pipe; Slice 2 adds operational resilience independently.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend the reconciler AC) asserting that the events-log URL used by reconcile.py includes the trailing slash, verified by an offline test that asserts the constructed URL or by inspecting the mock call args.
findings.finding: The Round-12 body specifies that the events-log query MUST use a trailing slash (`GET /a/plugins/events-log/events/`, 'without it, 404') as a verified fact, but no acceptance criterion tests or asserts this URL form — a silent 404 in the reconciler would trip no AC.
findings.location: Round-12 remediation body: 'events-log query uses the TRAILING SLASH'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit `aws_iam_role_policy` resource in `infra/` (following the S2/S4a inline-policy pattern) that grants the EC2 instance role `cloudwatch:PutMetricData` scoped to the `rebar/host` namespace, rather than relying on S1 having pre-provisioned this permission.
findings.finding: S4b's Round-12 remediation introduces `cloudwatch:PutMetricData` as a required IAM action on the EC2 instance role but claims this permission was 'established in S1' — S1's documented scope (EC2, EBS gp3, SSM SecureString, DLM) predates this voter-error metric requirement and does not include CloudWatch PutMetricData. If S4b's infra Terraform does not explicitly add this permission as a scoped inline policy, the boto3 PutMetricData calls will fail with AccessDenied, silently preventing the voter-error alarm from ever firing.
findings.location: Round-12 remediation section — 'On vote failure the receiver publishes `rebar/host:voter_errors` (boto3, instance role)… from S1'; Files > `infra/` bullet
findings.suggested_fix: In `infra/terraform/` (e.g. `voter_alarm.tf`), add: `resource "aws_iam_role_policy" "reviewer_cloudwatch" { name = "reviewer-cloudwatch" role = data.aws_iam_role.instance_role.name policy = jsonencode({ Version="2012-10-17", Statement=[{Effect="Allow", Action=["cloudwatch:PutMetricData"], Resource="*", Condition={StringEquals={"cloudwatch:namespace":"rebar/host"}}}] }) }`. This follows the existing S2/S4a inline-policy ownership pattern and scopes the grant to the specific namespace.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the CloudWatch metric namespace and metric name as a single authoritative constant (e.g. a Terraform `locals` value output to an SSM parameter or a receiver config env-var) consumed by both the `infra/` alarm resource and the receiver's boto3 call, so a divergence is a deployment-time configuration error rather than a silent INSUFFICIENT_DATA alarm.
findings.finding: The metric namespace/name used by the receiver's boto3 PutMetricData call (`rebar/host:voter_errors` in Round-12 prose) and the Terraform `aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm` resource in `infra/` are never tied to a shared constant or config value — a divergence between the two (different namespace capitalisation, metric name spelling, or dimension set) leaves the alarm permanently in INSUFFICIENT_DATA without any deployment-time error.
findings.location: Round-12 remediation section — 'increments a CloudWatch custom metric `rebar/host:voter_errors`'; Files > `infra/` — 'voter observability alarm (error-rate -> SNS) wiring'; AC 'On vote failure the receiver publishes `rebar/host:voter_errors`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the correct Gerrit REST endpoint for reading existing label votes on a revision (likely `GET /a/changes/{id}/revisions/{rev}/votes` returning a label→account→vote map, or `GET /a/changes/{id}/detail` with `LABELS` option) and replace the cited `GET /a/changes/{id}/revisions/current/review` with the verified endpoint in the gerrit_client.py spec.
findings.finding: The pre-vote authoritative idempotency check in Round-12 cites `GET /a/changes/{id}/revisions/current/review` as the endpoint to confirm whether the current revision already carries an LLM-Review vote from the bot. This is not a documented Gerrit REST GET endpoint — `SetReview` (POST /a/changes/{id}/revisions/{rev}/review) is write-only; the correct read endpoint for existing label votes is `GET /a/changes/{id}/revisions/{rev}/votes` (returns a label→[account→vote] map) or `GET /a/changes/{id}/detail` (includes labels). Using the wrong endpoint would return 404 or an unexpected response shape, causing the pre-vote check to always fail or always pass.
findings.location: Round-12 remediation — 'The AUTHORITATIVE idempotency guard is a Gerrit-side check BEFORE voting (`GET /a/changes/{id}/revisions/current/review` → does the current revision already carry an `LLM-Review` vote from the bot?)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'GET /a/changes/{id}/revisions/current/review' with 'GET /a/changes/{id}/revisions/current/votes' (returns {"LLM-Review": {"<bot-account-id>": <vote-value>}} if a vote exists) in the Round-12 remediation text and in the gerrit_client.py file spec.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the git transport and credentials used to perform the shallow checkout for `repo_root` (e.g. HTTPS using the bot's SSM token as HTTP Basic password, same credential as outbound Gerrit REST), and define the temp-directory lifecycle policy (e.g. per-review tmpdir under `/tmp` or a configured path, deleted atomically after the vote is cast or on worker error).
findings.finding: The plan requires the receiver to supply a valid `repo_root` (a fetched git checkout at the change ref) to `review_code()` / `code_review_decision()`, but never specifies the git transport credentials, temp-directory lifecycle, or cleanup strategy for that checkout — leaving the executor to design this from scratch.
findings.location: Round-10 remediation + Round-11 AC: 'The adapter calls review_code(..., source="local") (reads the already-cloned change-ref checkout in repo_root)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'The receiver clones the change ref via HTTPS using the bot's SSM-fetched HTTP password (`git clone --depth=1 --branch=refs/changes/...` or equivalent) into a `tempfile.mkdtemp()` directory that is deleted in a `finally` block after the vote is cast (success or failure). Credentials are not persisted to disk beyond the clone command.' This closes both the credential and lifecycle gaps with a single concrete policy.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace or augment the 'Fail-closed invariant' AC with a specific observable test scenario (e.g. 'an offline integration test that drops all webhooks AND disables the reconciler, then asserts that no change in the FakeGerrit has an LLM-Review=+MAX vote') so the invariant is evaluable in-session rather than asserted by prose.
findings.finding: The 'Fail-closed invariant' AC is framed as a system-wide invariant ('A missed event NEVER yields a submittable-but-unreviewed change') rather than a specific observable outcome with a defined test mechanism, making it unmeasurable as a standalone acceptance criterion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Fail-closed invariant' bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale for why the reconciler runs in-process as an asyncio background task (shared event loop, shared lock, shared SQLite connection avoids the need for a cross-process coordination mechanism that a separate-process reconciler would require at single-box scope).
findings.finding: The plan states that the backfill reconciler runs as an in-process asyncio background task (sharing the lock and SQLite connection with the webhook worker) but provides no affirmative rationale for WHY co-location is preferred over a separate process (e.g., systemd timer or cron job). The co-location is described only by its mechanical consequence ('they SHARE the lock + the SQLite connection'), not by a positive argument. A separate-process reconciler would require a cross-process coordination mechanism (a distributed lock, or accepting occasional double-review races mitigated only by the Gerrit-side pre-vote check); co-location avoids that complexity at single-box scope — but this reasoning is absent from the plan.
findings.location: Round-12 remediation — 'Single-flight lock (defined)' paragraph; Scope section; 'What' — Backfill reconciler bullet
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Backfill reconciler' bullet or the Round-12 single-flight definition, add: 'The reconciler runs as an in-process asyncio background task rather than a separate cron/systemd process so it shares the per-(change_id,revision) asyncio.Lock and the WAL-mode SQLite connection with the webhook worker — a separate process would require a cross-process lock (e.g., a SQLite exclusive transaction or a file lock) to prevent a backfill and a concurrent webhook retry from both passing the pre-vote Gerrit check and casting conflicting votes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a retirement/pruning policy for the SQLite `voted` table (e.g., DELETE rows older than N days, or on change-merged/abandoned events) and document it in the schema section or the dedup AC.
findings.finding: The SQLite `voted` table has no defined retirement or pruning path: rows for every processed (change_id, revision) accumulate indefinitely, with no age-based eviction, merge/abandon-triggered cleanup, or max-size policy stated anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: Round-12 remediation — SQLite schema / `src/rebar/review_bot/` dedup store
findings.suggested_fix: Add `voted_at INTEGER` (already in the schema) and a nightly or reconciler-triggered DELETE WHERE voted_at < epoch_now - 90_days; document this as the retention policy. Alternatively, note explicitly that the table is bounded by the project's change volume and no pruning is needed at expected scale — but make that an intentional, stated choice.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the Round-12 LIVE e2e AC to cover the BLOCK path: push a synthetic change with a known-blocking finding (or a pre-seeded diff that triggers the severity threshold), verify the receiver casts a real -1 LLM-Review vote on the running Gerrit, and confirm the change is NOT submittable.
findings.finding: The LIVE e2e AC (Round-12) only explicitly verifies the PASS→+MAX path ('a real patchset... becomes submittable'); there is no live e2e verification that a change with blocking findings receives a real -1 vote on the running Gerrit, leaving the BLOCK→-1 path validated only by offline FakeRunner tests.
findings.location: Round-12 plan-review remediation — 'LIVE e2e: a real patchset → webhook → review_code → LLM-Review vote → submittable'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the live e2e AC: 'AND a synthetic patchset whose diff contains a critical-severity finding (per the configured threshold) receives LLM-Review=-1 on the running Gerrit within one poll interval, verified via GET /a/changes/{id}/revisions/current/votes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a concurrency cap and/or inter-vote throttle (e.g. max N concurrent reviews, sleep M ms between batches) for the reconciler's catch-up pass so a large backfill does not burst-exhaust the LLM API or Gerrit REST rate limits.
findings.finding: The backfill reconciler's startup (catch-up) pass has no specified batching or rate-limiting: if the receiver has been offline for an extended period, the reconciler could attempt to concurrently review and vote on a large number of un-voted open changes, potentially overwhelming the LLM API, Gerrit REST, or the in-process worker pool.
findings.location: Backfill via reconciler section; `src/rebar/review_bot/reconcile.py`; Round-11 AC 'Reconciler is resumable+idempotent via a persisted cursor'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the reconciler spec: process un-voted patchsets in batches of at most N (e.g. 10) with a configurable inter-batch sleep (e.g. 2 s), using the same asyncio semaphore that bounds the webhook worker concurrency, so the catch-up pass is throttled identically to normal operation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consolidate the two single-flight lock ACs into one (or make R12 explicitly extend R11 by adding 'the same lock is shared with the backfill reconciler') to eliminate ambiguity about which test proves both properties.
findings.finding: The single-flight lock requirement is expressed as near-duplicate ACs across Round-11 and Round-12, creating ambiguity about which AC is authoritative and what its test scope is: Round-11 requires 'exactly one review execution + one vote' (concurrency test), while Round-12 requires the lock is 'shared by the webhook worker and the in-process backfill reconciler' (scope test). A reader verifying ACs could satisfy only one and believe compliance is achieved.
findings.location: Round-11 AC: 'Per-(change_id, revision) SINGLE-FLIGHT lock'; Round-12 AC: 'An in-process per-(change_id,revision) single-flight lock is shared by the webhook worker and the in-process backfill reconciler'
findings.suggested_fix: Merge into a single AC: 'An in-process per-(change_id,revision) asyncio.Lock shared by the webhook worker and the backfill reconciler prevents a second concurrent review; offline test: two concurrent deliveries (one from webhook path, one from reconciler) of the same revision yield exactly one review execution and one vote.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/review_bot/adapter.py` to the Files section with a description of its role (the d251↔b744 code_review_decision seam adapter).
findings.finding: `src/rebar/review_bot/adapter.py` is introduced as a named module with a formal public signature (`code_review_decision(diff_text, repo_root, ref) -> {decision: PASS|BLOCK}`) and is explicitly called out as 'the formal b744-WS6 seam', but it does not appear in the plan's 'Files' section — creating an orphan deliverable with no file-level traceability.
findings.location: Files section vs Round-11 remediation body: 'The formal d251↔b744 adapter seam is now concrete... code_review_decision(diff_text, repo_root, ref) -> {decision: PASS|BLOCK} in src/rebar/review_bot/adapter.py'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the canonical `robot_run_id` derivation formula (e.g. `sha256(hex(change_id + ':' + revision_id))[:16]`) in the idempotency section and in ADR-0009, so the d251 implementation and b744-WS6 produce identical IDs for the same (change, revision) pair.
findings.finding: The `robot_run_id` deterministic derivation formula is referenced but never defined. The plan says 'derive `robot_run_id` deterministically' as an idempotency strategy, but does not specify the input to the hash/formula (e.g. `sha256(change_id + revision + event_type)` or `f'{change_id}-{revision}'`).
findings.location: What section, 'Idempotency / dedup (at-least-once delivery)' bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the idempotency bullet: '`robot_run_id` = first 16 hex chars of `sha256(f"{change_id}:{revision_id}".encode())`. Record this formula in `docs/adr/0009-review-bot-pipe.md`.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
